title: Universal dynamics from a single-particle dark state
authors: Ruben Daraban, Arghavan Safavi-Naini, Johannes Schachenmayer, Mohammad Maghrebi
abstract: Open quantum systems can host dark or subradiant states whose decay is highly suppressed. While these states have been extensively studied in the few-excitation regime, their impact on the many-body dynamics remains largely unexplored. Here, we study a spin chain subject to correlated dissipation on neighboring sites, which admits a single-particle dark state at zero momentum. We show that the single-particle dark state qualitatively alters the many-body dynamics at long times, and identify its distinct universal behavior. While the zero-momentum mode is dark at the single-particle level, it decays slowly as $1/\log t$ as it becomes dressed by other modes through a dissipation-induced nonlinearity. We demonstrate that the momentum distribution takes a universal scaling form in $k\sqrt{t}$, and the total density decays as $1/\sqrt{t}\log t$. Our results further elucidate the origin of the conflicting results in recent works. Finally, we corroborate the analytics with matrix product state simulations and show that the same universal behavior persists for soft-core interactions, underscoring the universality of the emergent dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a single-particle dark state in a dissipative spin chain induces universal many-body dynamics characterized by logarithmic decay and specific scaling laws. By analyzing the nonlinear coupling between modes, the authors explain how the previously stable dark state eventually decays. The results are shown to be universal across both hard-core and soft-core bosonic models.
main_problem: Understanding the many-body dynamics of open quantum systems hosting dark or subradiant states and resolving conflicting reports regarding their long-time decay and critical exponents.
main_result: The study identifies a universal long-time decay of the total density as 1/(sqrt(t) log t) and a logarithmic decay of the single-particle dark state due to dissipation-induced nonlinearity, with a momentum distribution following a universal scaling form in k*sqrt(t).
method: The authors use a combination of Jordan-Wigner transformation, a time-dependent Generalized Gibbs Ensemble (GGE) ansatz, analytic continuation in the complex plane, and Matrix Product State (MPS) simulations.
model_or_system: An open XX spin chain (or hard/soft-core bosons) subject to correlated dissipation on neighboring sites, described by a Lindblad master equation with Lindblad operators L_j = sigma_{j+1}^- - sigma_j^-.
key_observables: Momentum distribution (rho_k and n_k), total density (n), zero-momentum mode decay (rho_0), and entanglement entropy.
important_parameters: Dissipation rate (Gamma), hopping (J), interaction strength (U), weak dissipation limit (Gamma << J), and long-time/scaling regime.
main_assumptions: Weak dissipation limit (dissipation timescale much longer than Hamiltonian timescale), thermodynamic limit (L -> infinity), and the use of a GGE ansatz for integrable systems.
figures_summary: The figures include a schematic of the spin chain, plots showing the crossover from 1/t to 1/log(t) decay in the zero-mode, scaling collapse of the momentum distribution, and verification of the algebraic tail and entanglement dynamics via MPS simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dark states in many-body systems, derives the nonlinear kinetic equations using a GGE approach and analytic continuation, presents the universal scaling laws for density and momentum, and concludes with numerical verification using MPS for both spin and bosonic models.
why_interesting: It provides a resolution to conflicting literature on dissipative spin chains and reveals how a single-particle symmetry-protected state can drive universal many-body dynamics through nonlinear dressing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
